criteria.label: Define precision, recall, and F1 with correct formulas.
criteria.example: Write: precision = TP/(TP+FP), recall = TP/(TP+FN), F1 = 2PR/(P+R), all at one stated threshold on labeled data.

criteria.label: Compute P/R/F1 from a given confusion matrix.
criteria.example: Given TP=12, FP=3, FN=5, TN=80: precision=0.80, recall=0.706, F1≈0.75 without using a library.

criteria.label: Explain the precision–recall trade-off when changing threshold.
criteria.example: State that lowering threshold from 0.6 to 0.4 usually increases recall and decreases precision because more predictions become positive.

criteria.label: Produce a sklearn classification report and interpret macro vs micro F1.
criteria.example: Run `classification_report` on a 3-class test set; note macro-F1=0.71 vs micro-F1=0.84 and identify the weakest class by per-row F1.

criteria.label: Choose and justify precision vs recall priority for a stated business scenario.
criteria.example: For spam filtering, argue for high precision ('no good mail in spam folder') and accept lower recall; cite asymmetric error costs.
